title: Collapse during a febrile illness, overnight

chart.oneLiner: 34M with a febrile flu-like illness presents after an abrupt nocturnal loss of consciousness, with a family history of unexplained sudden death.

opening: I came down with a fever yesterday, and then last night I just blacked out and woke up on the bathroom floor.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 34-year-old man, otherwise fit, now alert but feverish and shaken. Good historian.

HPI: ABRUPT loss of consciousness overnight with essentially NO prodrome (no nausea, no warmth, no tunnel vision beforehand). The episode happened while he was up at night during a febrile flu-like illness that started the day before. Rapid, complete recovery with NO post-event confusion. Briefly witnessed by his partner, who described a few jerks of the limbs lasting seconds AFTER he went down (convulsive syncope), then prompt return to normal. NO tongue-biting, NO incontinence, NO sustained shaking, NO post-ictal state. NO chest pain, NO palpitations he can clearly recall. He has had one or two prior unexplained brief blackouts at rest, never during exertion.
FAMILY HISTORY (only if asked): a brother died suddenly in his sleep in his 30s; an uncle drowned unexpectedly while swimming; no autopsy diagnosis was given. Strong family history of unexplained sudden death at a young age.
PMH: none; structurally normal heart by his account. MEDS: took an over-the-counter sodium-channel-affecting cold remedy and has been on no chronic medications (if asked). Recently took a fever reducer. SOCIAL: occasional alcohol, reports a heavy drinking episode preceding one prior blackout (if asked); no recreational drugs; non-smoker.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: alert, febrile, mildly diaphoretic, well-appearing between events.
- Vitals: BP 122/76, HR ~92 and regular, RR 16, Temp 38.6°C, SpO2 98% RA.
- Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm; normal S1/S2; NO murmurs, NO gallops; PMI not displaced; no signs of structural disease.
- Neuro: no focal deficit, fully oriented, no tongue laceration; gait normal.
- Volume/skin: euvolemic; no orthostatic drop on standing.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the family history of sudden death unless asked about family history or relatives dying young/suddenly.
- Do NOT volunteer the fever-precipitated timing, the nocturnal/at-rest pattern, or the brief post-fall jerks unless asked about the circumstances, warning, or what the witness saw.
- Do NOT volunteer the cold remedy / fever reducer or the prior alcohol-associated episode unless asked about medications or drinking.
- Emphasize (only if asked about prodrome/warning) that there was NO warning before the collapse.
- The cardiac and neuro findings are normal and revealed only if examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.Troponin: Normal serial troponins — no acute infarct pattern.
labs.BMP: Na 140, K 4.2 (normokalemic), Cr 0.9, Mg normal, glucose 96 — no metabolic cause for the event.
labs.Echo: Echocardiogram: structurally NORMAL heart; normal biventricular size/function, no RV dysplasia, no HCM, no critical valve lesion (excludes a structural arrhythmic substrate).
labs.FeverWorkup: Mild leukocytosis consistent with a viral febrile illness; the fever is the likely UNMASKING trigger rather than the primary problem.
labs.Genetics: SCN5A cardiac sodium-channel gene testing PENDING (supports the diagnosis but is not required to make it).

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Type 1 Brugada pattern: coved (downsloping) ST-segment elevation >=2 mm with a negative T wave in the right precordial leads V1-V2; QRS otherwise near-normal, no acute ischemic ST changes elsewhere.
ekg.verify: From the LITFL 'Brugada Syndrome' ECG library page — download an example tracing that matches: Type 1 coved ST elevation with a negative T wave in V1-V2. Self-host with credit to litfl.com.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing Type 1 Brugada pattern with coved ST elevation and negative T wave in V1-V2
searchTerms: coved ST elevation V1 V2 Brugada

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Normal chest radiograph: clear lung fields, normal cardiac silhouette (cardiothoracic ratio <0.5), no infiltrate, effusion, or pneumothorax.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: a normal PA chest radiograph with clear lungs and a normal cardiac silhouette. Self-host with appropriate credit.
cxr.imageDescription: normal PA chest radiograph with clear lungs and normal heart size

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this syncope (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Cardiac arrhythmic syncope due to a primary electrical (channelopathy) disorder — Brugada syndrome (leading & CANT-MISS,9). Ventricular tachyarrhythmia / VT-VF (CANT-MISS,4). Long QT syndrome / other inherited channelopathy (CANT-MISS,3). Structural disease (HCM / ARVC) (core,2). Convulsive syncope vs seizure (core,2). Recognizes that abrupt syncope without prodrome, a febrile/nocturnal trigger, and a family history of sudden death point to an inherited arrhythmia (core,5).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Cardiac arrhythmic syncope due to a primary electrical (channelopathy) disorder — Brugada syndrome. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Ventricular tachyarrhythmia (VT/VF); Long QT syndrome / other inherited channelopathy. Also reasonable: Structural disease (HCM/ARVC); Convulsive syncope vs seizure.

criticalActions.item: Cardiac arrhythmic syncope due to a primary electrical (channelopathy) disorder — Brugada syndrome
Long QT syndrome / other inherited channelopathy

coreActions.item: Recognizes that abrupt syncope without prodrome, a febrile/nocturnal trigger, and a family history of sudden death point to an inherited arrhythmia

mustNotMiss: Long QT syndrome / other inherited channelopathy

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: 12-lead EKG immediately, with high (2nd/3rd intercostal space) right precordial leads if suspicion is high (CANT-MISS — diagnostic,6). Continuous telemetry / cardiac monitoring (CANT-MISS,4). Echocardiogram to exclude structural disease (2). BMP incl. potassium/magnesium and glucose (2). Troponin to exclude ischemia (1). PENALTY: discharging or failing to place the patient on a monitor (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: 12-lead EKG immediately (with high right precordial leads if needed); Continuous telemetry / cardiac monitoring; Echocardiogram to exclude structural disease.

criticalActions.item: 12-lead EKG immediately (consider high right precordial leads)

penalties.item: discharging or failing to place the patient on a monitor

requiredTests: 12-lead EKG immediately (consider high right precordial leads)
contraindicatedTests: discharging or failing to place the patient on a monitor
orderSensitiveActions: discharging or failing to place the patient on a monitor

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings (be specific about the right precordial leads).
steps.rubric: Findings = Type 1 Brugada pattern. CREDIT: identifies coved ST-segment elevation in V1-V2 (6); recognizes the negative/inverted T wave following the coved ST (3); names it as a Type 1 Brugada pattern (4); systematic read with pertinent negatives — no acute ischemia, normal QT (2). PENALTY: dismissing it as benign early repolarization / normal, or over-calling an anterior STEMI (-3).
keyFindings: negative T wave following the coved ST segment
names it as a Type 1 Brugada pattern
systematic read, no acute ischemia elsewhere
criticalFindings: Type 1 Brugada pattern: coved ST-segment elevation >=2 mm with a negative T wave in the right precordial leads V1-V2.
commonMistakes: dismissing the right precordial changes as benign early repolarization or a normal variant
steps.idealAnswer: Type 1 Brugada pattern: coved (downsloping) ST-segment elevation >=2 mm with a negative T wave in the right precordial leads V1-V2; no acute ischemic changes elsewhere and a normal QT interval.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Type 1 Brugada pattern — coved ST-segment elevation with a negative T wave in V1-V2.

coreActions.item: negative T wave following the coved ST segment
names it as a Type 1 Brugada pattern
systematic read, no acute ischemia elsewhere

penalties.item: dismissing it as benign early repolarization/normal or over-calling an anterior STEMI

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = no acute process. CREDIT: identifies a normal film with a normal cardiac silhouette (6); notes it helps exclude structural/pulmonary causes of the syncope (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: notes the normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs help exclude structural causes
criticalFindings: Normal chest radiograph: clear lungs, normal cardiac silhouette, no acute process.
commonMistakes: inventing an infiltrate or cardiomegaly on a normal film
steps.idealAnswer: Normal chest radiograph: clear lung fields and a normal cardiac silhouette (cardiothoracic ratio <0.5); no acute process, which helps exclude a structural or pulmonary cause for the syncope.

criticalActions: Normal chest radiograph: clear lungs, normal cardiac silhouette, no acute process.
coreActions: notes the normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs help exclude structural causes

penalties.label: inventing pathology on a normal study

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the coved ST elevation in V1-V2, a structurally normal heart, syncope unmasked by fever, and a family history of sudden death, what is your diagnosis and why is it dangerous?
steps.rubric: Brugada syndrome (a sodium-channelopathy) (6). Explains the danger — risk of polymorphic VT/VF and sudden cardiac death, especially with fever or sodium-channel-blocking drugs; explicitly notes the heart is structurally normal so the substrate is electrical (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Brugada syndrome (a sodium channelopathy). Dangerous because it carries a risk of polymorphic VT/VF and sudden cardiac death, which is heightened by fever and by sodium-channel-blocking drugs; the heart is structurally normal, so the arrhythmic substrate is purely electrical.
mustNotMiss: Long QT syndrome / other inherited channelopathy

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH syncope / inherited arrhythmia: continuous telemetry monitoring with admission (CANT-MISS,4); aggressive fever control with antipyretics as fever unmasks/triggers arrhythmia (CANT-MISS,6); cardiology/EP consult for risk stratification and ICD in this high-risk (syncope + Type 1 pattern) patient (CANT-MISS,5); stop/avoid Brugada-aggravating drugs — sodium-channel blockers, certain antiarrhythmics, excess alcohol (3); counsel on family screening / genetic evaluation given inherited risk (3); treat the precipitating febrile illness (2). PENALTY: discharging an unmonitored patient with syncope and a Type 1 Brugada pattern (-4); giving a sodium-channel-blocking antiarrhythmic (e.g., procainamide/flecainide) as treatment (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Admit with continuous telemetry. Control fever aggressively with antipyretics (fever unmasks the Type 1 pattern and provokes arrhythmia). Cardiology/EP consult for risk stratification — given syncope plus a spontaneous/fever-induced Type 1 pattern, this is high-risk and an ICD is indicated. Stop and avoid drugs that aggravate Brugada (sodium-channel blockers, certain antiarrhythmics, tricyclics, excess alcohol). Counsel on family screening and genetic evaluation. Treat the underlying febrile illness. For electrical storm, isoproterenol/quinidine are the rescue agents.

criticalActions.item: Cardiology/EP consult for risk stratification and ICD (high-risk: syncope + Type 1 pattern)

coreActions.item: Stop/avoid Brugada-aggravating drugs (sodium-channel blockers, certain antiarrhythmics, excess alcohol)
Counsel on family screening / genetic evaluation

penalties.item: Discharging an unmonitored patient with syncope and a Type 1 Brugada pattern
Giving a sodium-channel-blocking antiarrhythmic as treatment

requiredActions: Cardiology/EP consult for risk stratification and ICD (high-risk: syncope + Type 1 pattern)
optionalActions: Stop/avoid Brugada-aggravating drugs (sodium-channel blockers, certain antiarrhythmics, excess alcohol)
Counsel on family screening / genetic evaluation
unsafeActions: Discharging an unmonitored patient with syncope and a Type 1 Brugada pattern
Giving a sodium-channel-blocking antiarrhythmic as treatment

triggerConcepts: heart problems run in the family
historyTriggers.response: My older brother died suddenly in his sleep in his 30s, and an uncle drowned out of nowhere while swimming. Nobody ever gave us a clear reason why.
No warning at all — no nausea, no dizziness, nothing. One moment I was up, the next I was on the floor.

triggerConcepts: did it happen with a fever
historyTriggers.response: It happened overnight, while I was up at night with a fever from this flu I caught the day before. I wasn't exerting myself — I was just walking to the bathroom.
My partner saw it. She said I dropped, had a few quick jerks of my arms for a couple of seconds after I went down, then came right back to normal. No tongue-biting and I didn't wet myself.
I came around almost immediately and felt clear-headed — no grogginess or confusion afterward.
I've had one or two brief blackouts before, always when I was at rest, never during exercise. I never got them checked out.
No chest pain, and I don't remember my heart racing. No shortness of breath either.
I'm not on any regular medicines. For this flu I took an over-the-counter cold remedy and a fever reducer over the last day.
I drink occasionally. I did have a heavy night of drinking right before one of those earlier blackouts, now that you mention it.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 34M with a febrile flu-like illness presents after an abrupt nocturnal loss of consciousness, with a family history of unexplained sudden death. The diagnosis is Brugada syndrome, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm; normal S1/S2; NO murmurs, NO gallops; no signs of structural disease.
Cardiac: PMI not displaced; no signs of structural disease.
Neuro: no focal deficit, fully oriented, no tongue laceration; gait normal.
Volume/skin: euvolemic; no orthostatic drop on standing.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Brugada syndrome while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Brugada syndrome and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
commonPitfalls: Discharging an unmonitored patient with syncope and a Type 1 Brugada pattern
Giving a sodium-channel-blocking antiarrhythmic as treatment

Normal chest radiograph (helps exclude structural/pulmonary causes).

Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.
